In short, the cosine distance can be expressed in terms of Euclidean distance as [...] Another effective proxy for cosine distance can be obtained by L 2{\displaystyle L_{2}}Image 26: {\displaystyle L_{2}} normalisation#Euclidean_norm "Norm (mathematics)") of the vectors, followed by the application of normal Euclidean distance. Using this technique each term in each vector is first divided by the magnitude of the vector, yielding a vector of unit length. Then the Euclidean distance over the end-points of any two vectors is a proper metric which gives the same ordering as the cosine distance (a monotonic transformation of Euclidean distance; see below) for any comparison of vectors, and furthermore avoids the potentially expensive trigonometric operations required to yield a proper metric. Once the normalisation has occurred, the vector space can be used with the full range

title: Cosine Distance, Dot Product versus Euclidean in vector similarity search | Data Science Collective
snippet:
Let u be a vector `[x,y]`. By definition, u.u = x\x + y\y = x¬≤+ y¬≤. Dot product of a vector with self is the sum of squares of the coordinates.
 We also know that the L2 magnitude is the square root of the sum of squares of the coordinates i.e. ||u|| = ‚ąö(x¬≤+ y¬≤). So u.u = ||u||¬≤
 Now consider 2 vectors u & v. The difference is u-v. From the above bullet, we know that ||u-v||¬≤ = (u-v) . (u-v) which is ||u||¬≤ ‚ąí 2u.v + ||v||¬≤
 If u & v are L2 normalized vectors, then ||u|| & ||u|| are 1. We also know that in case of normalized vectors, the dot product & the cosine similarity are interchangeable. So the RHS is reduced to 2 ‚ąí 2cos\_sim(u,v). Thus, ||u-v|| = ‚ąö(2 ‚ąí 2cos\_sim(u,v)). So the L2 or the Euclidean distance between any 2 normalized vectors = ‚ąö( 2 ‚ąí 2cos\_sim(u,v)) or ‚ąö( 2 ‚ąí u.v). [...] Press enter or click to view image in full size

The dot product between u & v vectors is the product of the magnitude of the projection of  u onto v and the magnitude of v i.e. ||u||cosőł||v|| or ||u|| ||v|| cosőł.

We thus arrive at a generic formula for dot product: ‚ą•u‚ą• ‚ą•v‚ą•cosőł. This formula is applicable for all scenarios, irrespective of whether vectors are in same direction or not! When the vectors are pointed in the same direction, cosőł is 1 & the dot product has the maximum possible value. When they are orthogonal, the dot product is 0. So in that sense, higher the dot product, greater the similarity. In Physics, when a force F moves an object along displacement d, the work done is the dot product ‚ą£‚ą£F‚ą£‚ą£ ‚ą£‚ą£d‚ą£‚ą£cos‚Ā°őł. [...] As we can right away make out, Cosine similarity is simply the dot product divided by the product of the magnitudes of the 2 vectors. If we L2-normalize the vectors in question, then cosine similarity & the dot product are exactly the same! Normalizing a vector means dividing the vector by its magnitude. In other words the magnitude of a normalized vector is 1. It may sound un-intuitive but it is often convinient to normalize vectors in deep learning.

How are both of these related to the Euclidean distance?
